food_subgroup: Mixed seafood platter with grilled and breaded components

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a mixed grilled-and-breaded seafood platter; closest_parent 'seafood dish' (FOODON_03301116) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Fish and seafood products) is the closest defensible parent category for a mixed seafood platter; no exact composite-platter code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 'Seafood (food)' is the closest parent concept; no specific mixed-platter concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One seafood platter serving is approximately 450 g, comprising portions of grilled salmon, breaded fish, grilled prawns, breaded calamari rings, and blue shell mussels with dipping sauces.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Salmon (cooked, dry heat), Breaded fish portions (frozen, baked), Shrimp (cooked), Squid (fried), Mussels (cooked, moist heat)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a mixed seafood platter of this composition. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of five USDA FoodData Central entries reflecting the described components: grilled salmon (~25% by weight), breaded fish (~20%), grilled prawns (~20%), breaded calamari (~20%), and blue shell mussels (~15%). Dipping sauces contribute minor carbohydrate and sodium. Weights are approximate and reflect a typical restaurant platter composition. Values are per 100 g of the composite edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on breaded/fried fish and seafood entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a mixed seafood platter. GI estimated at 38 based on the weighted contribution of components: grilled salmon and prawns contribute negligible glycemic load (GI ~0 for pure protein-fat foods, assigned GI 5 for calculation purposes); breaded fish and calamari rings contribute the dominant glycemic component, with breaded/fried fish GI reported at approximately 38–45 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Mussels contribute minimal carbohydrate. Composite GI of 38 reflects the low-GI character of the predominantly protein-and-fat platter with moderate breaded carbohydrate content. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 33.75) / 100 ≈ 12.8, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 7.5 × 4.5 = 33.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from breading, dipping sauces, and natural seafood sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that this platter contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,710 mg per full 450 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board; requesting sauces on the side and limiting sauce consumption can meaningfully reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high protein and fat content, with moderate carbohydrate from breading
type2_diabetes.guidance: The platter has a low estimated glycemic index of 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 12.8 per serving, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the breaded components contribute refined carbohydrates and the dipping sauces may contain added sugars, so portion control of breaded items and sauce selection remain important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon and mussels, with moderate saturated fat from breaded components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The grilled salmon and mussels in this platter provide cardioprotective long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; the breaded components add saturated fat and trans fat, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour the grilled items and limit the breaded portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Seafood Platter is a mixed seafood sharing dish served at Singaporean casual-dining and Western-influenced restaurants. As described at #Foodcoholic (40 Circular Road, Singapore), the platter comprises five seafood components: grilled Atlantic salmon fillets, breaded white fish pieces, grilled tiger prawns, breaded calamari rings, and blue shell mussels, accompanied by dipping sauces. The grilled components are cooked over direct heat, yielding a lightly charred exterior with moist, flaky or firm flesh, while the breaded items are coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried to a golden, crispy texture. The mussels are steamed or grilled in their shells. The overall flavour profile is savoury and oceanic, with contrasting textures of crispy breading, tender grilled fish, and plump shellfish. Nutritionally, the platter is high in protein (approximately 17.8 g per 100 g), provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from the salmon and mussels, and contains moderate fat (7.2 g per 100 g) with a relatively low glycemic index of approximately 38 due to the dominant protein-and-fat composition. Sodium is elevated due to the breading, sauces, and natural seafood sodium content. The dish is not suitable for individuals with fish, crustacean, or mollusc allergies, and contains gluten from the breadcrumb coating. It is appropriate for pescatarians and is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and vitamin D. Clinically, the platter is a moderate-sodium, low-GI, omega-3-rich meal that is broadly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk when the breaded components and dipping sauces are consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: What is in a seafood platter?
Is seafood platter good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact mixed seafood platter composition. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of five USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) entries corresponding to the described components. The GI is an analogous estimate based on breaded fish and seafood entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the component proportions are assumed from typical restaurant platter compositions and are not confirmed by the serving restaurant.
notes: 1. Cuisine reclassified from 'Indian' (input tag) to 'Singaporean' because the dish description (grilled salmon, breaded fish, prawns, calamari, mussels with dipping sauces) is a Western-influenced seafood platter served at a Singapore restaurant; the 'Indian' and 'Chicken Dish' cuisine_tags in the input are inconsistent with the dish content and are not carried forward. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation, portion sizes, and sauce composition. 3. Halal status is marked false because the restaurant (#Foodcoholic) is not confirmed halal-certified; this should be verified against the restaurant's current certification. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries and are not individually reported for this specific platter; these values carry higher uncertainty than the core macros. 5. No sentinel -1 values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields are populated with composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI set to 38 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database breaded/fried fish entries; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 7. GL per 450 g serving calculated as (38 × 33.75) / 100 = 12.8 g.

Assigned GI 38 (low) from analogous University of Sydney GI Database estimate. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Populated all health_context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Reclassified cuisine from 'Indian' to 'Singaporean' with documented justification. Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only permitted strings. Confirmed no -1 sentinel values used (all micronutrients estimated from USDA). Confirmed GI category 'low' consistent with GI=38 ≤55 threshold.
